title: How the whole app holds together
summary:
**Default.** `next.config.ts` boots a Next.js app with workflow support, PDF tracing, Sentry instrumentation, and the app router shell.

**Path.** `RootLayout` resolves `resolveRequestAccessContext()`, passes tenant/demo/user state into `PathnameLayoutShell`, and the obra workspace fans out into document, extraction, generation, and reporting routes.

**Invariant.** The app stays coherent when tenant access, storage paths, lineage keys, generated PDFs, and workflow side effects all preserve the same `tenantId` and `obraId` boundaries.

markers.label: Next runtime configuration
markers.description: `next.config.ts` is the runtime boundary for the app. It enables React strict mode and React Compiler, pins the Turbopack root, traces Chromium files for `/api/pdf-render`, and raises the server action body limit for large document-generation payloads.

markers.label: Workflow and Sentry wrapper
markers.description: `buildConfig()` decides whether `withWorkflow()` wraps the Next config, then conditionally wraps production Vercel builds with Sentry. This is where background workflow support joins the app before deployment.

markers.label: Root layout resolves access
markers.description: `RootLayout` creates the request-scoped Supabase client and calls `resolveRequestAccessContext()` before rendering the shell. This is the first central join between cookies, user identity, tenant selection, demo access, and UI state.

markers.label: Cookie-backed Supabase client
markers.description: `createClient()` builds the server-side Supabase client from request cookies and `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_*` configuration. Server components can read cookies, while cookie writes are best-effort because some calls happen from Server Components.

markers.label: Service-role Supabase client
markers.description: `createSupabaseAdminClient()` is the explicit service-role bypass. It obtains `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` through `requireVersionedSecret()` and disables persisted auth sessions, so callers need a clear server-only reason to use it.

markers.label: Access context chooses user or demo
markers.description: `resolveRequestAccessContext()` is the investigation target for the whole app. It returns a normal user context when Supabase auth has a user, otherwise it resolves a demo session and swaps to an admin-backed Supabase client with a synthetic demo user and membership.

markers.label: Layout shell receives app state
markers.description: `PathnameLayoutShell` receives the user, roles, document permissions, tenants, demo session, and demo capabilities. This is the server-to-client handoff that makes navigation, permissions, and visible app state consistent after access resolution.

markers.label: Admin configuration surfaces
markers.description: `app/admin` owns tenant and superadmin configuration surfaces: users, roles, defaults, document flows, macro tables, tenant secrets, demo links, and tenant management. Its local rules require tenant resolution and admin/superadmin checks before these configuration screens mutate database-backed behavior.

markers.label: Excel page initial data
markers.description: `getExcelPageInitialData()` reuses `resolveRequestAccessContext()` for the obras list. It refuses anonymous access, checks demo capability for `excel`, and loads tenant main-table configuration and obras together.

markers.label: Excel list hydrates client state
markers.description: `DesktopExcelPageClient` receives server-loaded table config and obras, then maps the obra rows into the client table state. This is the main portfolio surface before the user drills into a single obra.

markers.label: File manager uploads document
markers.description: `FileManager` sends selected files to `/api/obras/${obraId}/documents/upload` with `file` and `folderPath` in `FormData`. The UI expects the route to return the final storage path and possibly renamed file name after collision handling.

markers.label: Upload route gates tenant access
markers.description: `POST /documents/upload` resolves access, rejects anonymous callers, checks demo `excel` capability, requires `tenantId`, and verifies the `obra` belongs to the current tenant and is not soft-deleted before touching storage.

markers.label: Upload route writes storage
markers.description: The upload route checks tenant storage budget, generates a collision-safe storage path, writes the file to the `obra-documents` bucket, and records upload metadata in `obra_document_uploads` when a real user is present.

markers.label: Upload route tracks document
markers.description: `obra_document_uploads` stores the bucket, storage path, file name, and uploader. The response returns the normalized path and file name the file manager uses to update its tree.

markers.label: OCR import gates tenant access
markers.description: `POST /tablas/import/ocr-multi` follows the same access pattern as upload. It resolves access, keeps the Supabase client for later writes, rejects anonymous callers, and gates demo sessions on the `excel` capability before reading table or document state.

markers.label: Lineage key derivation
markers.description: `deriveLineageRowKeys()` prefers a business key when configured, otherwise derives structural keys from compacted parent/item data and normalized content fingerprints. This is the domain identity layer that lets imports update rows instead of creating duplicates.

markers.label: OCR import upserts lineage rows
markers.description: The OCR route derives `lineage_row_key`, reads existing materialization versions, prepares `obra_tabla_rows`, and upserts on `tabla_id,lineage_row_key`. This preserves stable row identity across repeated extractions from the same document contract.

markers.label: Document generation gates access
markers.description: `POST /api/document-generation/generate` resolves the same access context, creates an admin client for storage writes, rejects anonymous callers, and loads document-generation permissions before rendering a document.

markers.label: Document generation calls PDF renderer
markers.description: The generation route renders template HTML, POSTs it to `/api/pdf-render`, converts the PDF response to bytes, then uploads the resulting PDF to `obra-documents` under either the existing generated-document path or a collision-safe new path.

markers.label: PDF renderer launches Chromium
markers.description: `POST /api/pdf-render` accepts HTML, resolves Puppeteer launch options, starts Chromium, loads the HTML with `domcontentloaded`, and returns a PDF buffer. This route is the clearest candidate for a future internal Vercel Service because it carries the heavy Chromium runtime.

markers.label: Obra completion emits event
markers.description: When an obra crosses completion logic in the bulk route, the API emits `obra.completed` with a prepared context. Completion is not just data persistence; it can trigger notification and workflow side effects.

markers.label: Notification engine starts workflow
markers.description: `emitEvent()` expands notification effects, resolves conditional fields and scheduled dates, filters disabled effects, and starts `deliverEffectsWorkflow()` through the workflow runtime.

markers.label: Workflow delivers effects
markers.description: `deliverEffectsWorkflow()` runs as a workflow, sleeps until scheduled delivery times when needed, then writes in-app notifications or sends email via workflow-safe helpers. This closes the loop from obra events back to users.
